title: Perioperative desmopressin prolongs survival in surgically treated bitches with mammary gland tumours: a pilot study
note: Evidence is CONFLICTING: positive prospective pilot (809 vs 237 d) and a positive grade-2/3 study (PMID 21164169), but a later randomized trial found no benefit (PMID 32441479, Vet Comp Oncol 2020); a feline mastectomy study also exists (Vet Comp Oncol 2021).

title: Adjuvant Thalidomide and Metronomic Chemotherapy for the Treatment of Canine Malignant Mammary Gland Neoplasms
note: 58 dogs across 4 treatment arms; no overall survival difference, benefit seen ONLY in the distant-metastasis subgroup (p<0.0001), and allocation/randomization is not clearly described.

title: In vitro and in vivo antiproliferative activity of metformin on stem-like cells isolated from spontaneous canine mammary carcinomas: translational implications for human tumors
note: Only cell-line and xenograft work on canine mammary tumour cells; I found NO clinical trial of metformin in live pet dogs with mammary carcinoma.

Sunitinib for RCC is human standard of care, not repurposing; I found NO dog/cat RCC study of sunitinib - the TKI actually used for canine RCC is the related toceranib (Palladia), e.g. Front Vet Sci 2023 case report PMC10680256.

title: Piroxicam therapy in 34 dogs with transitional cell carcinoma of the urinary bladder
note: CAVEAT on the label: this NSAID->anticancer repurposing succeeded so well that piroxicam (alone or with mitoxantrone/carboplatin) is now the accepted first-line medical therapy for canine bladder TCC, so calling it 'not standard therapy' is questionable today even though it remains off-label.

disease_name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of Head and Neck
title: Evaluation of piroxicam for the treatment of oral squamous cell carcinoma in dogs
note: 17 dogs with oral SCC on piroxicam monotherapy: 1 CR, 2 PR, 5 SD; genuinely off-label here since standard of care for canine oral SCC is surgery/radiation, and combination piroxicam+carboplatin data also exist (PMID 19379210).

title: Losartan Blocks Osteosarcoma-Elicited Monocyte Recruitment, and Combined With the Kinase Inhibitor Toceranib, Exerts Significant Clinical Benefit in Canine Metastatic Osteosarcoma
note: Antihypertensive repurposed via CCL2-CCR2 blockade; 28 dogs with metastatic OS, 50% clinical benefit rate, but given in combination with toceranib so losartan's solo contribution is not isolated.

title: Adjuvant Sirolimus Does Not Improve Outcome in Pet Dogs Receiving Standard-of-Care Therapy for Appendicular Osteosarcoma: A Prospective, Randomized Trial of 324 Dogs
note: Sirolimus was genuinely repurposed and rigorously tested in dogs, but this definitive 324-dog RCT was NEGATIVE (no DFI or survival benefit) - literature confirms the repurposing attempt but refutes efficacy, hence WEAK not SUPPORTED.

title: Auranofin improves overall survival when combined with standard of care in a pilot study involving dogs with osteosarcoma
note: Single-arm multicentre pilot (40 dogs, historical control n=26) adding auranofin (an antirheumatic gold drug) to amputation+carboplatin; survival benefit mainly in males, so efficacy is suggestive rather than definitive.

title: Use of ketoconazole to treat dogs with pituitary-dependent hyperadrenocorticism: 48 cases (1994-2007)
note: Clear repurposing (antifungal used off-label as a steroidogenesis inhibitor; standard therapy is trilostane/mitotane); retrospective 48-dog series, 90% clinical improvement.

title: Efficacy and side effects of radiation therapy in comparison with radiation therapy and temozolomide in the treatment of measurable canine malignant melanoma
note: Bi-institutional trial, 15 dogs RT-only vs 12 dogs RT+temozolomide; response rates similar but time to progression significantly longer with temozolomide (205 vs 110 d, p=0.046); small and not randomized (owner-elected chemotherapy).

title: Does thalidomide prolong survival in dogs with splenic haemangiosarcoma?
note: 15 dogs post-splenectomy, MST 172 d with 33% surviving >1 y; single-arm with no concurrent control, supported mechanistically by VEGF immunostaining work (PMID 32443710, Vet Sci 2020).

title: ACVIM consensus statement: Guidelines for the identification, evaluation, and management of systemic hypertension in dogs and cats
note: ACVIM consensus explicitly states amlodipine besylate is the first-choice antihypertensive in cats; supporting RCTs also exist.

title: Efficacy of long-term oral telmisartan treatment in cats with hypertension: Results of a prospective European clinical trial
note: Multicenter placebo-controlled RCT (n=285 cats); telmisartan (Semintra) is FDA-approved for feline systemic hypertension.

title: Treatment of naturally occurring asthma with inhaled fluticasone or oral prednisolone: A randomized pilot trial
note: Glucocorticoids are first-line for feline asthma (prednisolone preferred over prednisone due to poor feline oral bioavailability of prednisone); also documented in the Merck Veterinary Manual.

title: Systemic Treatment of Inflammatory Airway Disease in Animals - Merck Veterinary Manual
note: Merck Vet Manual lists feline dosing (3 mg/kg PO q12h), but efficacy in cats is variable and ex vivo work found theophylline the least potent bronchodilator tested; used more in dogs, so adjunct rather than standard of care.

title: Systemic Treatment of Inflammatory Airway Disease in Animals - Merck Veterinary Manual
note: Merck Vet Manual gives explicit feline dosing (0.01 mg/kg SC for crises; 0.625 mg/cat PO q12h chronic); standard as rescue/adjunctive bronchodilator, never as sole therapy without a glucocorticoid.

title: Treatment of canine atopic dermatitis: 2015 updated guidelines from the International Committee on Allergic Diseases of Animals (ICADA)
note: ICADA guidelines recommend oral prednisolone/prednisone 0.5 mg/kg q12-24h to induce remission, then taper to lowest effective dose.

title: Treatment of canine atopic dermatitis: 2015 updated guidelines from the International Committee on Allergic Diseases of Animals (ICADA)
note: ICADA guidelines recommend oral ciclosporin 5 mg/kg q24h for chronic canine AD; also FDA-approved as Atopica, with supporting placebo-controlled RCTs.

title: Postoperative adjuvant treatment of invasive malignant mammary gland tumors in dogs with doxorubicin and docetaxel
note: Doxorubicin IS given to dogs with mammary carcinoma, but this prospective trial concluded 'chemotherapy did not lead to an improved outcome'; surgery is the standard and adjuvant benefit is unproven — the 標準 label is not supported.

title: Acute Evolution of Neurological Signs and Magnetic Resonance Imaging Features in Dogs With Brain Tumors Receiving Treatment With Corticosteroids and Anticonvulsants
note: Corticosteroids are 'a principal component of palliative care for humans and animals with brain tumors for over seven decades'; standard as palliation of peritumoral edema (53% clinical improvement), NOT antitumor therapy.

title: Neoplasia of the Nervous System in Small Animals - MSD Veterinary Manual
note: MSD Vet Manual states 'Hydroxyurea is the chemotherapeutic agent of choice for meningiomas' in dogs (20 mg/kg PO q24h), but same source notes 'few studies exist in which dogs received it as the sole adjuvant therapy'; supporting canine data are case reports (e.g. PMID 18176033) and PMID 34216463.

title: Neoplasia of the Nervous System in Small Animals - MSD Veterinary Manual
note: MSD Vet Manual: 'Addition of cytotoxic drugs (most commonly hydroxyurea or lomustine [CCNU]) postoperatively or at recurrence may lengthen survival time' (30-90 mg/m2 PO q4-6wk); doubt flagged - a 2023 review (PMID 38026627) states no standard regimen exists and lomustine-alone survival was comparable to palliative care.

title: Gabapentin: Clinical Use and Pharmacokinetics in Dogs, Cats, and Horses
note: Widely used off-label as a multimodal adjunct but no placebo-controlled trial isolates gabapentin's benefit in canine OA; review calls for proper RCTs, so not standard of care as monotherapy.

title: Effects of meloxicam on severity of lameness and other clinical signs of osteoarthritis in dogs
note: Randomized placebo-controlled multicenter trial (217 dogs); meloxicam is a licensed first-line NSAID for canine OA.

title: Lack of effectiveness of tramadol hydrochloride for the treatment of pain and joint dysfunction in dogs with chronic osteoarthritis
note: Evidence points the OTHER way: a blinded placebo-controlled crossover RCT found tramadol (5 mg/kg q8h) gave no clinical benefit in canine OA; commonly prescribed off-label but not supported as standard of care.

title: Immune-Mediated Polyarthritis in Dogs and Cats - Merck Veterinary Manual
note: Merck Vet Manual states 'Initial treatment of IMPA in dogs typically involves immunosuppression with corticosteroids (eg, prednisolone or prednisone)'; canine RA is a rare erosive subset of IMPA for which prognosis is guarded.

title: Recognizing and treating immune-mediated polyarthritis in dogs
note: No primary canine literature found; this review states methotrexate 'has not been evaluated for erosive IMPA in dogs' and is only recommended by some authors in refractory cases, and the Merck Vet Manual IMPA page does not list it at all - clearly off-label extrapolation from human RA, not standard of care.

Sulfasalazine is a standard human RA DMARD but I found no veterinary literature supporting it for canine rheumatoid arthritis; in dogs it is used for chronic/ulcerative colitis and large-bowel IBD. The Merck Vet Manual IMPA article lists corticosteroids, azathioprine, cyclosporine and leflunomide - sulfasalazine is not mentioned.

title: Efficacy of leflunomide for treatment of immune-mediated polyarthritis in dogs: 14 cases (2006-2008)
note: Merck Vet Manual states 'Leflunomide can also be used instead of steroids, with good results' and this case series showed 93% complete/partial response; caveat - evidence is for IMPA broadly not erosive RA specifically, and a 2024 retrospective (93 dogs) found no improved outcomes when added to glucocorticoids.

title: Effect of an orally administered sulfonylurea, glipizide, for treatment of diabetes mellitus in cats
note: Real feline use with trial evidence and feline DM is type-2-like, but efficacy is poor (only ~25% good responders) and AAHA guidelines recommend glipizide only for cats whose owners refuse insulin — insulin is the standard of care; glipizide must not be used in dogs.

title: Clinical Outcome of Multicentric Lymphoma Treated with Cyclophosphamide, Doxorubicin, Vincristine, and Prednisolone (CHOP) in Small Breed Dogs
note: This protocol explicitly used prednisolone (not prednisone) as the CHOP steroid; prednisolone is standard in canine lymphoma protocols.

title: Early progression during or after cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone chemotherapy indicates poor outcome with rescue protocols in dogs with multicentric lymphoma
note: Prednisone is the 'P' of CHOP; also standard with vincristine for canine acute lymphoblastic leukemia.

title: Dose escalation study to evaluate safety, tolerability and efficacy of intravenous etoposide phosphate administration in 27 dogs with multicentric lymphoma
note: NOT standard of care and DISEASE MISMATCH: the only canine efficacy evidence is for multicentric (mature B-cell) lymphoma, not precursor lymphoblastic leukemia-lymphoma; canine ALL/LBL is treated with CHOP +/- L-asparaginase (etoposide absent from published protocols and from the one canine lymphoblastic lymphoma case report, PMID 37732143). Oral etoposide bioavailability in dogs is also low and erratic (median 13.4%, AJVR 2008).

title: Toxicity of cytarabine constant rate infusion in dogs with high-grade non-Hodgkin lymphoma with bone marrow or central nervous system involvement
note: Supports the curator's narrower '標準CNS淋巴瘤' label (cytarabine crosses the blood-brain barrier and is used for CNS/bone-marrow involvement); important caveat: single-agent cytarabine was NOT active for systemic remission induction in canine lymphoma (PMID 7983628), so it is standard only for the CNS indication.

title: Intrathecal chemotherapy for the management of lymphoblastic lymphoma in a 4-year-old dog: a case report
note: Exact disease match (canine lymphoblastic lymphoma) with intrathecal methotrexate + cytarabine, but this is a single case report (n=1) explicitly described by the authors as a first report; methotrexate is otherwise only an old LMP/UW-Madison maintenance component, so 標準 is overstated.

title: Early progression during or after cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone chemotherapy indicates poor outcome with rescue protocols in dogs with multicentric lymphoma
note: Doxorubicin is the 'H' of CHOP, the accepted first-line standard for canine lymphoma; caveat: MeSH D054198 is precursor/lymphoblastic disease whereas most canine lymphoma treated with CHOP is mature B-cell, so the disease mapping is a proxy.

title: Early progression during or after cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone chemotherapy indicates poor outcome with rescue protocols in dogs with multicentric lymphoma
note: Vincristine is the 'O' (Oncovin) of CHOP and is also used with prednisone in canine acute lymphoblastic leukemia; same proxy caveat on the precursor-cell disease mapping.

title: Response rate to a single dose of vinblastine administered to dogs with treatment-naive multicentric lymphoma
note: Evidence actively contradicts the 標準 label: vinblastine was 'minimally efficacious' front-line in canine lymphoma and the authors do not recommend it; it appears only in rescue protocols (e.g. MVPP). Likely curator confusion with vincristine, which IS the lymphoma vinca.

title: Evaluation of single-agent mitoxantrone as chemotherapy for relapsing canine lymphoma
note: Genuine canine lymphoma agent but the authors concluded single-agent mitoxantrone has 'limited value' at 1st relapse; it is an alternative to doxorubicin (CMOP protocol) rather than first-line standard.

title: Evaluation of a high-dose chemotherapy protocol with no maintenance therapy for dogs with lymphoma
note: Only indirect support: this abstract confirms chlorambucil was a crossover/maintenance agent in the classic UW-Madison canine lymphoma protocol, but modern standard CHOP omits maintenance; chlorambucil's real niche is indolent lymphoma/CLL, not high-grade precursor lymphoblastic disease, so the 標準 label looks overstated.

title: Lomustine (CCNU) for the Treatment of Resistant Lymphoma in Dogs
note: Real and accepted canine use but as a RESCUE agent for relapsed/resistant lymphoma (28% response), not first-line standard of care; no PMID surfaced in my searches so the field is left empty rather than guessed (DOI verified).

title: Chronic myelogenous leukemia in the dog
note: DISEASE MISMATCH: the veterinary evidence (and the record's own Chinese label) is for CHRONIC myeloid leukemia, not the mapped MeSH D015470 (ACUTE myeloid leukemia); in 5 dogs hydroxyurea lowered WBC counts but was 'of questionable value in the prevention of leukemic blast crisis'; canine AML is treated with cytarabine-based protocols, so hydroxyurea is not standard of care for AML.

title: Canine chronic enteropathy - Current state-of-the-art and emerging concepts
note: Azathioprine is a second-line adjunct to glucocorticoids in canine chronic inflammatory enteropathy/IBD (the canine analogue of ulcerative colitis), not first-line; efficacy studies are lacking and one study found prednisolone+chlorambucil superior. Contraindicated in cats (cannot metabolize it).

title: Randomized controlled trial demonstrates nutritional management is superior to metronidazole for treatment of acute colitis in dogs
note: Widely used historically for canine colitis, but this RCT found diet SUPERIOR to metronidazole (remission 5 vs 8.5 days) with worsened dysbiosis, so current evidence contradicts standard-of-care status; also MeSH 'Colitis, Ulcerative' is a human entity with no exact canine equivalent.

title: Randomized, controlled trial of budesonide and prednisone for the treatment of idiopathic inflammatory bowel disease in dogs
note: Real canine RCT (40 dogs) showing budesonide equivalent to prednisone for IBD, but evidence is for canine chronic enteropathy/IBD generally rather than 'ulcerative colitis'; budesonide is an accepted alternative/second-line steroid, not first-line standard.

title: Drugs Used to Treat Inflammatory Bowel Disease in Monogastric Animals - MSD Veterinary Manual
note: MSD Vet Manual: sulfasalazine (10-30 mg/kg PO q8-12h) is commonly used in dogs for ulcerative/idiopathic/plasmacytic-lymphocytic colitis after dietary causes excluded; note it is an established colitis drug but diet and glucocorticoids are first-line, and canine chronic colitis is only an analogue of human ulcerative colitis.

title: Prognostic factors for multiple myeloma in the dog
note: 60 dogs treated with melphalan plus prednisone (43% CR, 49% PR); prednisone is standard only as part of the melphalan+corticosteroid combination, not as monotherapy.

title: Comparison of two melphalan protocols and evaluation of outcome and prognostic factors in multiple myeloma in dogs
note: Melphalan is the established chemotherapy backbone for canine multiple myeloma (median survival 930 d here; also Matus PMID 3721983).

title: ACVIM consensus statement: Support for rational administration of gastrointestinal protectants to dogs and cats
note: ACVIM consensus explicitly designates PPIs as standard of care for medical treatment of gastroduodenal ulceration/erosion in dogs and cats; dose twice daily.

title: ACVIM consensus statement: Support for rational administration of gastrointestinal protectants to dogs and cats
note: Widely used in dogs/cats but the ACVIM consensus states H2RA monotherapy is INFERIOR to twice-daily PPI for ulceration, so famotidine is second-line rather than standard of care.

title: ACVIM consensus statement: Support for rational administration of gastrointestinal protectants to dogs and cats
note: ACVIM consensus states PPIs are superior to sucralfate for gastroduodenal ulceration; sucralfate is adjunctive (mainly esophagitis) with no proven added benefit alongside PPIs.

title: A review of the pharmacology of carbonic anhydrase inhibitors for the treatment of glaucoma in dogs and cats
note: CAIs are 'principal drugs used in the management of canine and feline glaucoma', but MSD Vet Manual says acetazolamide commonly causes anorexia and methazolamide 'is the drug of choice'; topical dorzolamide has largely superseded systemic acetazolamide. Note canine glaucoma is predominantly closed-angle - open-angle is limited to breeds like Beagles.

title: Comparison of the effects of topical administration of a fixed combination of dorzolamide-timolol to monotherapy with timolol or dorzolamide on IOP, pupil size, and heart rate in glaucomatous dogs
note: Same Beagle primary open-angle glaucoma trial; timolol is standard in canine glaucoma mainly as the fixed dorzolamide-timolol combination — timolol monotherapy was the least effective arm, so 'standard' applies to the combination more than to timolol alone.

title: Comparison of the effects of topical administration of a fixed combination of dorzolamide-timolol to monotherapy with timolol or dorzolamide on IOP, pupil size, and heart rate in glaucomatous dogs
note: Exact disease match — study used 12 laboratory Beagles with inherited primary open-angle glaucoma; dorzolamide lowers IOP and is a mainstay of canine glaucoma medical therapy, though the dorzolamide-timolol combination outperformed dorzolamide alone.

title: Effect of different dose schedules of latanoprost on intraocular pressure and pupil size in the glaucomatous Beagle
note: Exact disease match — 8 Beagles with moderate-stage inherited primary open-angle glaucoma, significant IOP reduction; note latanoprost does NOT lower IOP in healthy cats (PMID 11039551), so this is dog-only support.

No veterinary literature found treating canine/feline 'depression' as a diagnosis with fluoxetine; vets diagnose anxiety/compulsive disorders, not depression, so this MeSH mapping appears spurious despite fluoxetine's strong anxiety evidence.

title: Cutaneous lupus erythematosus in dogs: a comprehensive review
note: Review recommends glucocorticoids across CLE variants - oral prednisone/prednisolone at 2 mg/kg/day, with lesions responding best to immunosuppressive doses and remission generally within one month.

Methotrexate is used for human SLE/cutaneous lupus but is not mentioned anywhere in the 2018 BMC Vet Res comprehensive review of canine cutaneous lupus erythematosus, and I found no canine lupus reports; canine therapy is glucocorticoids +/- azathioprine, ciclosporin, tacrolimus, tetracycline-niacinamide.

title: Concurrent 5-fluorouracil and carboplatin for the treatment of canine carcinomas
note: 5-FU is used in canine carcinomas (24 dogs, 43% response in gross disease) and in canine GI adenocarcinoma (PMID 15552318, 3 dogs), but neither abstract confirms colorectal cases specifically; surgery is standard for canine colorectal tumors. CRITICAL: 5-FU is fatal/contraindicated in cats.

title: Spotlight on capecitabine for the treatment of unresectable or metastatic carcinoma of various origin: A retrospective study of 25 dogs
note: Off-label/investigational salvage use, NOT standard of care in dogs: in 25 dogs with advanced carcinoma where conventional therapy failed or was declined, 2 partial responses were in dogs with inoperable colic/colorectal adenocarcinoma; a pilot PK study (PMID 38297307) calls 750 mg/m2 only 'a reasonable starting dose for future phase 1 dose finding trials'. Capecitabine is standard for colorectal cancer in HUMANS, which is likely the source of this edge.

title: Oral glucocorticoid pulse therapy for induction of treatment of canine pemphigus foliaceus - a comparative study
note: Glucocorticoids (prednisone/prednisolone 2-4 mg/kg q24h) are the undisputed mainstay/first-line induction therapy for canine and feline pemphigus foliaceus; note conventional monotherapy induced complete remission in only 15% of dogs at 3 months vs 61% with pulse therapy, hence adjuvants are usually added. Study design (prospective vs retrospective comparison) not confirmed - evidence_level is my best estimate.

title: The use of oclacitinib compared to azathioprine in the management of canine pemphigus foliaceus: A retrospective analysis
note: Azathioprine is the most commonly used steroid-sparing adjuvant for canine pemphigus foliaceus (13/15 dogs achieved partial or complete remission, mean 77.9% reduction in glucocorticoid dose); DOG ONLY - azathioprine is myelotoxic and contraindicated in cats.

title: A Retrospective Evaluation of the Steroid-Sparing Effect of Oral Modified Ciclosporin for Treatment of Canine Pemphigus Foliaceus
note: Recognised glucocorticoid-sparing adjuvant for pemphigus foliaceus: 9/11 dogs reached complete remission on ciclosporin + glucocorticoids and 5/9 discontinued steroids; a feline retrospective (Irwin, Vet Dermatol 2012, DOI 10.1111/j.1365-3164.2012.01069.x) reports the same sparing effect in cats. Caveat: ciclosporin MONOtherapy was ineffective in 4/5 dogs in a pilot study, so it is an adjuvant rather than a first-line single agent.

title: Combined Gemcitabine and Carboplatin Therapy for Carcinomas in Dogs
note: 37 dogs with mixed carcinomas including 9 respiratory (2 pulmonary carcinoma, 5 bronchoalveolar); overall response only 13% with one neutropenic-sepsis death; vinorelbine is the better-established first-line for canine primary pulmonary carcinoma - gemcitabine is off-label and not standard. PMID not seen, left empty.

title: 2016 AAFP Guidelines for the Management of Feline Hyperthyroidism
note: Unambiguous standard of care; AAFP guidelines specify methimazole 1.25-2.5 mg/cat q12h titrated to T4 1.0-2.5 mcg/dL.

title: Clinical trial of vinblastine in dogs with transitional cell carcinoma of the urinary bladder
note: Real antitumor activity (36% PR in 28 dogs) but framed by the authors as 'another treatment option'; an accepted second-line/rescue agent rather than first-line standard.

title: Randomized phase III trial of piroxicam in combination with mitoxantrone or carboplatin for first-line treatment of urogenital tract transitional cell carcinoma in dogs
note: Randomized phase III first-line trial; mitoxantrone plus piroxicam is an accepted first-line protocol for canine bladder TCC (also PMID 12576466, Clin Cancer Res 2003).

title: Metronomic administration of chlorambucil for treatment of dogs with urinary bladder transitional cell carcinoma
note: Prospective trial in 31 dogs, but explicitly a salvage option after other treatments failed (only 3% PR, 67% stable disease) — not first-line standard of care.

title: Effects of reconcile (fluoxetine) chewable tablets plus behavior management for canine separation anxiety
note: Fluoxetine (Reconcile) is FDA-approved for canine separation anxiety; placebo-controlled trial supports efficacy with behavior modification.

title: Treatment of separation anxiety in dogs with clomipramine: results from a prospective, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group, multicenter clinical trial
note: Standard-dose clomipramine plus behavior therapy beat placebo; clomipramine (Clomicalm) is FDA-approved for canine separation anxiety.

title: ACVIM consensus statement on the treatment of immune-mediated hemolytic anemia in dogs
note: Glucocorticoids (prednisolone/prednisone) are the ACVIM-endorsed first-line cornerstone of canine IMHA therapy.

title: Methylprednisolone alone or combined with cyclosporine or mycophenolate mofetil for the treatment of immune-mediated hemolytic anemia in dogs, a prospective study
note: Glucocorticoids are first-line for canine IMHA per the 2019 ACVIM consensus statement (PMID 30847984); this prospective trial used methylprednisolone specifically as the backbone agent.

title: ACVIM consensus statement on the treatment of immune-mediated hemolytic anemia in dogs
note: ACVIM consensus lists azathioprine as a reasonable second immunosuppressive agent in dogs; not recommended in cats.

title: ACVIM consensus statement on the treatment of immune-mediated hemolytic anemia in dogs
note: ACVIM consensus lists cyclosporine among the reasonable second-agent choices (5-10 mg/kg PO q12h) alongside glucocorticoids.

title: ACVIM consensus statement on the treatment of immune-mediated hemolytic anemia in dogs
note: ACVIM consensus lists mycophenolate mofetil as a reasonable second immunosuppressive agent; consensus advises against combining it with azathioprine.

title: Surgical treatment of canine nasal aspergillosis by rhinotomy combined with enilconazole infusion and oral itraconazole
note: Caveat: itraconazole is the oral agent of choice for disseminated canine aspergillosis, but for sinonasal disease oral monotherapy has limited efficacy and topical clotrimazole/enilconazole with debridement is first-line.

title: Oral administration of voriconazole with surgical fungal plaque debridement for the treatment of sinonasal aspergillosis with cribriform plate lysis in three dogs
note: Only a 3-dog case series; voriconazole is a salvage/second-line option (notably for cribriform lysis), not standard of care, and is poorly tolerated in cats.

disease_name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of Head and Neck
title: Toxicoses and efficacy associated with administration of mitoxantrone to cats with malignant tumors
note: Mitoxantrone has been given to cats with oral SCC (32 cats in this series) but response was poor; no source found supporting it as standard of care for head/neck SCC in dogs or cats.

disease_name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of Head and Neck
title: Electrochemotherapy in treatment of canine oral non-tonsillar squamous cell carcinoma. A case series report
note: Bleomycin is used for canine/feline oral SCC only as the cytotoxic partner in electrochemotherapy (12-dog case series); systemic bleomycin is not a standard-of-care therapy for this tumor.

title: Clinical efficacy and tolerability of zonisamide monotherapy in dogs with newly diagnosed idiopathic epilepsy: Prospective open-label uncontrolled multicenter trial
note: Zonisamide is an accepted canine antiseizure drug (76% of 53 dogs had >=50% seizure reduction), but 2015 ACVIM graded it Level III/Grade C — weaker than phenobarbital — and I found no data specific to juvenile myoclonic epilepsy.

title: 2015 ACVIM Small Animal Consensus Statement on Seizure Management in Dogs
note: Phenobarbital is Level I/Grade A first-line monotherapy for canine epilepsy generally; it was among the AEDs used in the 21 Rhodesian Ridgeback juvenile myoclonic epilepsy dogs (PMID 28223533) but levetiracetam/KBr appeared more effective, so support is for canine epilepsy broadly rather than JME specifically.

title: Absence Seizures as a Feature of Juvenile Myoclonic Epilepsy in Rhodesian Ridgeback Dogs
note: Disease-specific match: canine JME (DIRAS1, Rhodesian Ridgeback); levetiracetam gave 97.2% EEG-confirmed myoclonic seizure reduction and abolished absence seizures, and was rated most effective in the 21-dog cohort (PMID 28223533) — but evidence is one EEG-documented case plus owner-reported series, not a controlled trial.

title: ACVIM Consensus Statement on the management of status epilepticus and cluster seizures in dogs and cats
note: IV bolus diazepam is a first-line emergency option (ACVIM Recommendation A in dogs, B in cats), but this supports seizure-emergency rescue in general, not juvenile myoclonic epilepsy specifically; diazepam CRI is graded D in cats and should be avoided.

title: Hypoparathyroidism in Dogs - Musculoskeletal System - MSD Veterinary Manual
note: MSD Veterinary Manual gives explicit calcitriol dosing (0.02-0.03 mcg/kg PO q24h loading, then 0.005-0.015 mcg/kg q24h) as lifelong therapy for hypoparathyroidism.

title: Clinical efficacy of a benazepril and spironolactone combination in dogs with congestive heart failure due to myxomatous mitral valve disease: The BEnazepril Spironolactone STudy (BESST)
note: Double-blind RCT (569 dogs) showed added spironolactone reduced risk of cardiac death/worsening; 2019 ACVIM guidelines separately recommend spironolactone as an adjunct for chronic Stage C heart failure.

title: ACVIM consensus guidelines for the diagnosis and treatment of myxomatous mitral valve disease in dogs
note: Guideline states a loop diuretic (furosemide or torasemide) with pimobendan is the correct medical treatment for Stage C heart failure in dogs; furosemide is unambiguously standard of care.

title: Clinical efficacy of a benazepril and spironolactone combination in dogs with congestive heart failure due to myxomatous mitral valve disease: The BEnazepril Spironolactone STudy (BESST)
note: Benazepril was the backbone ACE-inhibitor therapy in both arms of this 569-dog RCT; 2019 ACVIM guidelines (PMID 30974015) name benazepril as an ACEI option for Stage C heart failure.

title: Controlled clinical evaluation of enalapril in dogs with heart failure: results of the Cooperative Veterinary Enalapril Study Group
note: Placebo-controlled RCT (211 dogs, MR/DCM) with all 14 clinical variables improved; 2019 ACVIM MMVD guidelines (PMID 30974015) also direct 'continue or start ACEI (eg, enalapril or benazepril)' in Stage C.

title: ACVIM consensus guidelines for the diagnosis and treatment of myxomatous mitral valve disease in dogs
note: Guideline recommends digoxin in Stage C heart failure ONLY when complicated by persistent atrial fibrillation, to slow ventricular rate — it is not standard therapy for canine heart failure per se, so the 標準 label is too broad.

title: Canine osteosarcoma. Treatment by amputation versus amputation and adjuvant chemotherapy using doxorubicin and cisplatin
note: Amputation plus doxorubicin/cisplatin roughly doubled median survival (300 vs 175 d); doxorubicin-based adjuvant chemo is established standard of care in canine appendicular OSA.

title: Drug-eluting bead chemoembolization for the treatment of nonresectable hepatic carcinoma in dogs: A prospective clinical trial
note: Only solid canine evidence is doxorubicin delivered by drug-eluting bead TACE for NONRESECTABLE HCC (85% stable disease/partial response, 13 dogs, 2 treatment-related deaths); surgery is the treatment of choice and systemic doxorubicin is reported as unsatisfactory - not standard of care.

title: Islet Cell Tumors in Dogs and Cats - Merck Veterinary Manual
note: Merck Vet Manual states 'Glucocorticoids, such as prednisolone (0.25 mg/kg, PO, every 12 hours longterm), are an alternative to diazoxide'; source names prednisolone specifically, though prednisone is used interchangeably in dogs.

title: Prospective evaluation of biweekly streptozotocin in 19 dogs with insulinoma
note: Real prospective canine trial (median PFS 196 d, OS 308 d) and the only chemo agent with demonstrated antitumor activity, but reserved as adjuvant for metastatic/recurrent disease - Merck notes 'high risk of severe adverse effects' (diabetes mellitus in 8/19) and does not recommend it as primary treatment.

title: Islet Cell Tumors in Dogs and Cats - Merck Veterinary Manual
note: Merck Vet Manual states 'Diazoxide ... is the preferred drug for treating insulinomas'; extra-label but the accepted first-line medical management of canine insulinoma hypoglycemia (controls signs in 59-85% of dogs).

title: Erythrocytosis (Polycythemia) in Animals - Merck Veterinary Manual
note: Merck Vet Manual: 'Periodic phlebotomy with or without extra-label administration of hydroxyurea (30 mg/kg q24h PO for 7-10 days, then 15 mg/kg q24h, titrated to lowest effective dose) has been advocated in affected dogs and cats' - standard alongside phlebotomy for primary erythrocytosis/PV.

title: Combination chemotherapy of canine and feline cryptococcosis using subcutaneously administered amphotericin B
note: Standard for severe/CNS/disseminated cryptococcosis, typically combined with an azole; azole monotherapy suffices for milder disease.

title: Cryptococcosis in cats: clinical and mycological assessment of 29 cases and evaluation of treatment using orally administered fluconazole
note: Fluconazole is a first-line oral agent for feline cryptococcosis (excellent response in 29 cases); ABCD feline guideline also covers this indication.

title: Mycobacterioses in Cats: ABCD guidelines on prevention and management
note: ABCD (European Advisory Board on Cat Diseases) guideline recommends 'clarithromycin 125 mg/cat q24h ... for 6-9 months' as part of 2-3 drug combination therapy for the NTM group, and lists clarithromycin as first-line for MAC infection; evidence base is feline (dogs less well documented), and ~37% clarithromycin resistance is reported among mycobacterial isolates so susceptibility testing is advised.

No veterinary literature found for RIFABUTIN specifically in dogs or cats: the ABCD feline mycobacterioses guideline (PMID 23813823), the Merck Vet Manual rifamycins page and the VetTimes feline mycobacteriosis review all recommend RIFAMPICIN (rifampin) and none mentions rifabutin at all. Likely a human-MAC/HIV-derived edge, or drug-class confusion with rifampicin.

title: Mitotane (o,p'-DDD) treatment of 200 dogs with pituitary-dependent hyperadrenocorticism
note: >80% of dogs treated beyond 3 months had good-to-excellent response; mitotane is an established canine Cushing's therapy, though trilostane is now generally preferred first-line in current practice.

title: The use of liposomal amphotericin B in the management of Xylohypha bantiana mycosis in a dog
note: DOUBT: no chromoblastomycosis-specific dog/cat evidence found; the cited case is phaeohyphomycosis (a related but distinct dematiaceous-fungal entity). LABEL MISMATCH: evidence_label says blastomycosis, for which amphotericin B IS standard in severe disease.

title: South African report of first case of chromoblastomycosis caused by Cladosporium (syn Cladophialophora) carrionii infection in a cat with feline immunodeficiency virus and lymphosarcoma
note: LABEL MISMATCH: evidence_label says blastomycosis (D001759) but MeSH D002862 is confirmed Chromoblastomycosis; the only feline chromoblastomycosis case found had itraconazole treatment explicitly FAIL, so this is not standard of care for the stated disease.

title: Diabetes Insipidus in Animals - Merck Veterinary Manual
note: Merck Vet Manual states 'The primary treatment for central diabetes insipidus is desmopressin acetate'; lifelong therapy, extra-label but universally accepted.

title: Histoplasmosis in Animals - Infectious Diseases - Merck Veterinary Manual
note: Merck Veterinary Manual states verbatim that itraconazole (10 mg/kg q24h) is the treatment of choice for disseminated histoplasmosis in dogs and cats.

title: Pneumocystosis in dogs: meta-analysis of 43 published cases including clinical signs, diagnostic procedures, and treatment
note: Trimethoprim-sulfonamide is the most commonly used and most successful therapy for canine pneumocystosis (11/17 treated dogs survived); authors conclude prompt TMP-sulfonamide plus low anti-inflammatory corticosteroid doses substantially increases survival. Caveat: trimethoprim is always given as the TMP-sulfonamide COMBINATION, never as a single agent.

title: LeishVet guidelines for the practical management of canine leishmaniosis
note: Allopurinol is standard first-line therapy but only in combination (with meglumine antimoniate or miltefosine); LeishVet does not recommend allopurinol monotherapy.

title: ACVIM consensus statement on the diagnosis and treatment of chronic hepatitis in dogs
note: ACVIM consensus recommends UDCA for dogs with chronic hepatitis showing cholestasis; Merck Vet Manual canine cholangiohepatitis page states ursodiol may benefit dogs and cats with any cause of intrahepatic cholestasis (10-15 mg/kg/day) - off-label but standard adjunctive choleretic, contraindicated in complete biliary obstruction.

title: Evaluation of the use of chemotherapy and other prognostic variables for surgically excised canine thyroid carcinoma with and without metastasis
note: Doxorubicin is commonly given for canine thyroid carcinoma (reported response rates 30-50%), but this retrospective study found no significant survival benefit over surgery alone (518 vs 510 days) and 'no variable tested (... doxorubicin versus platinum ...) had a significant impact on survival'; surgery is the standard. DOI not shown on source.

title: Mitotane treatment of 32 dogs with cortisol-secreting adrenocortical neoplasms
note: Established medical alternative to adrenalectomy for canine cortisol-secreting adrenocortical tumors (66% good-to-excellent response); trilostane is now a common co-equal alternative.

title: Chemotherapy of canine hemangiosarcoma with doxorubicin and cyclophosphamide
note: Small (n=16) study vs historical controls, but doxorubicin-based adjuvant protocols after splenectomy are widely described as standard of care for canine HSA; survival gains are modest.

title: ACVIM consensus statement on the diagnosis and treatment of chronic hepatitis in dogs
note: Consensus states 'D-penicillamine is the Cu chelator of choice' at 10-15 mg/kg BID; MeSH term is human Wilson disease, the canine counterpart being copper-associated hepatopathy/chronic hepatitis.

title: Secondary prevention of cardiogenic arterial thromboembolism in the cat: The double-blind, randomized, positive-controlled feline arterial thromboembolism; clopidogrel vs. aspirin trial (FAT CAT)
note: FAT CAT RCT showed clopidogrel superior to aspirin for preventing recurrent CATE; 2020 ACVIM consensus (PMID 32243654) recommends clopidogrel in cats at risk of ATE - thromboprophylaxis adjunct to HCM, not HCM therapy itself.

title: ACVIM consensus statement guidelines for the classification, diagnosis, and management of cardiomyopathies in cats
note: The '標準貓HCM' label is contradicted by the 2020 ACVIM consensus: 'atenolol has not been shown to have any effect on the 5-year survival rate in cats with subclinical HCM'; it only 'may be considered' for stage B1 with severe DLVOTO or for ventricular ectopy/AF rate control, all at low level of evidence.

title: Evaluation of the calcium channel-blocking agents diltiazem and verapamil for treatment of feline hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
note: Real feline HCM data exist (1991 JVIM trial, 12 cats) but the 2020 ACVIM consensus (PMID 32243654) does NOT endorse diltiazem for routine HCM management - only 'may be considered' for atrial fibrillation rate control; historical use, not current standard.

title: ACVIM consensus statement on the diagnosis and treatment of chronic hepatitis in dogs
note: Corticosteroids are the accepted mainstay for idiopathic/presumed immune-mediated canine chronic hepatitis once copper and infection are excluded, but the consensus is cautious ('short courses of an anti-inflammatory dosage of corticosteroids may be beneficial') and no prospective trials exist.

title: ACVIM consensus statement on the diagnosis and treatment of chronic hepatitis in dogs
note: Consensus names azathioprine as an option some panelists combine with corticosteroids, but explicitly says none of these agents 'have been evaluated in prospective clinical trials' and 'not enough evidence is available to recommend an optimal immunosuppressive protocol' - adjunct, not standard.

title: European consensus document on mast cell tumours in dogs and cats
note: Consensus-level source covering both dogs and cats; corticosteroids are a core component (vinblastine + prednisolone most commonly used). Primary data for the steroid alone used prednisone, not prednisolone (PMID 7884726, 20% response as sole agent).

title: Efficacy of vinblastine for treatment of canine mast cell tumors
note: Vinblastine + prednisolone is the most commonly used conventional chemo for canine mast cell disease (European consensus PMID 22882486); caveat: trial evidence is for cutaneous grade II/III mast cell TUMOUR, whereas MeSH D007946 is the rare disseminated mast cell leukemia form, so the disease mapping is a proxy.

title: Chlorambucil and prednisolone chemotherapy for dogs with inoperable mast cell tumours: 21 cases
note: Real canine use (38% response) but positioned as a metronomic ALTERNATIVE for dogs unsuitable for surgery/radiotherapy, not first-line standard; also listed as an option in feline MCT though never evaluated prospectively in cats.

title: Treatment of canine mast cell tumors with CCNU (lomustine)
note: 42% measurable response; authors concluded CCNU 'should be considered an active agent' for canine MCT, and consensus protocols use CCNU alone or alternating with vinblastine; same mast-cell-tumour vs mast-cell-leukemia proxy caveat.
